data.title: Sky's Dual Transformation: Governance Upgrade and On-Chain Institutional Infrastructure
data.description:
Author: Jae, PANews

While DeFi lending leader Aave is mired in the Kelp DAO hack, another established protocol, Sky, has not only attracted significant investment from whales, with its TVL surging over 25% in the past two weeks, but has also capitalized on this momentum by launching two major initiatives to pave the way for its institutionalization strategy through governance reforms:

Internally, the protocol has proposed simplifying its treasury management mechanism, shifting the spending model from manual governance voting to hard rule constraints;

Externally, it is building Laniakea, an institutional-grade on-chain capital allocation infrastructure, attempting to capture a share of the 300 billion idle stablecoin liquidity market.

Sky is accelerating its efforts to secure a niche in the DeFi new infrastructure ecosystem.

From Community-Driven "Rule by Man" to Rule-Based "Rule of Law" On April 25th, Sky founder Rune Christensen posted on a governance forum, stating that the asset transfer from Genesis Capital to Grove was complete, officially concluding the Genesis phase of the protocol. During the Genesis phase, Sky adopted a human-driven governance approach: community voting determined expenditures, and funds were allocated discretionarily, providing sufficient flexibility for the early expansion of the ecosystem. However, as the asset size exceeded tens of billions of dollars, the resulting uncertainty and high governance costs gradually became a constraint on the protocol's creditworthiness. The most direct signal was S&P Global's B- credit rating for Sky. S&P Global had directly pointed out Sky's weaknesses: uncontrollable governance risks and opaque capital positions. For a protocol carrying billions of dollars in stablecoin credit, governance uncertainty is itself a significant systemic risk. Sky's solution is to streamline and restructure the Treasury Management Function (TMF). The protocol reduces the complex 5-step waterfall structure to a 4-step fixed framework. The most important constraint is the protocol's hard cap on operating expenditures. Sky shifts from rule by man to rule by law, confining treasury power within the cage of code. In the old system, the community's discretionary power over Step 1 was capped at 21% during the Genesis phase, with the Post-Genesis phase originally planned to be between 4-10%. However, if the percentage was variable, each adjustment required complex governance voting. Therefore, the new proposal directly overturns the entire old system, permanently locking the expenditure percentage at 20%. This means governance friction will be significantly reduced, and at least 80% of the protocol's net revenue will remain within the system for reserve accumulation, token burning, or distribution to holders. For SKY holders and ecosystem partners, a fixed expenditure percentage will be more predictable than highly uncertain governance decisions. The 20% rigid expenditure will make the treasury's cash flow more transparent and harder to manipulate through governance. In essence, while Sky has proactively reduced its governance, it has also offered a pledge of "certainty." Building an Institutional-Grade On-Chain Capital Operating System. While internally amending its constitution, Sky is also opening its doors to external stakeholders. On April 28th, Sky announced it was building Laniakea, a standardized infrastructure framework for institutional-grade capital deployment, for its Sky Agent Network, aiming to address the idle over $300 billion in the stablecoin market. It's important to note that this "Agent" is not the same as the commonly understood AI Agent. Sky Agent refers to Capital Agent, not the AI ​​Agent as commonly understood. The Sky team believes that the main reason why institutional funds have been hesitant to enter the market for so long is the lack of five key elements: shared infrastructure, standardized smart contracts, risk assessment, data systems, and legal framework. Laniaakea attempts to bridge the infrastructure gap through standardization across four dimensions: Smart contract standardization: template-based deployment, saving institutions the cost of reinventing the wheel; Risk governance standardization: unified risk measurement standards, with losses shared sequentially; Data infrastructure standardization: protocol encoding will be stored in a machine-readable format, supporting real-time AI risk control; Legal compliance standardization: providing a pluggable identity and KYC registration system, a shared legal framework across product lines, and an accountability mechanism for collateral support at each operational level. Under the Laniakea architecture, Sky will no longer be a "lender," but a network platform of capital agents. Primes: These are the primary agents (Sky Agents), similar to on-chain fund managers, competing for capital allocation quotas and developing investment strategies according to Laniakea's unified standards. Examples include Spark, responsible for DeFi lending, and Grove, responsible for private lending and RWA. Halos: These are Primes, specific financial products incubated based on Laniakea's shared infrastructure, covering various yield streams from government bond RWA to private lending. This layered architecture allows Sky to integrate the expertise of different agents for diversified asset allocation while maintaining a unified framework, thereby significantly improving the scalability of the ecosystem. In other words, based on Laniakea, Sky's role will shift from a "direct operator" to a standardized on-chain operating system designed for institutional capital. PANews believes the protocol's main revenue will come from stability fees, interest rate spreads, and taxes. Stability fees are Sky's most traditional and stable way to generate profit. Whenever Halos managed by Primes wants to be used as collateral to mint USDS on Sky, it needs to pay interest to Sky, which is the stability fee. Laniakea lowers the barrier to institutional access, meaning more institutional assets will enter the system. As the total amount of USDS minted increases, the total amount of stability fees earned by the protocol will also increase. Primes, as a professional asset manager, is responsible for bringing yield strategies to Sky. The protocol will provide liquidity for assets through USDS at a lower cost. Sky earns the net spread between the "strategy yield" and the "USDS funding cost (such as deposit rates)". Laniakea's standardization allows Sky to manage hundreds of spread channels simultaneously, creating economies of scale. Each individual Prime is essentially a "franchise" of Sky. Generally, Prime issues its own tokens, requiring it to pay a certain percentage of these tokens or a portion of its business revenue to Sky. Even if the protocol doesn't directly participate in issuing a specific product segment, as long as Primes are Halos issued based on Laniakea, Sky can generate revenue through taxation. It's worth noting that because the protocol state is machine-readable, AI will handle functions such as capital allocation and liquidation management. By reading Laniakea's standardized data interface, AI can achieve real-time monitoring of cross-asset exposure, collateral quality, and liquidity depth. When a certain underlying collateral exhibits risk signals such as abnormal interest rate differentials, AI can automatically adjust the credit limit or liquidation threshold of the corresponding Halos according to preset "machine rules," providing institutions with algorithmic-level capital security protection. Furthermore, machine readability transforms Halos into "standardized Lego" that can be optimized by AI models. AI can automatically switch capital allocations between different risk levels based on market interest rates and volatility, seeking the optimal Sharpe ratio. Overall, Laniakea's AI compatibility will empower institutional capital or Primes at the risk management and investment decision-making levels. Positioning itself at the infrastructure level, but the transformation harbors three hidden concerns. Sky's two actions are not independent but rather a combined strategy. A rule-based treasury management mechanism provides governance certainty for institutional capital, while Laniakea provides technological certainty. Sky's actions also reflect a logical shift taking place in the entire DeFi market: from competition at the application layer ("front-end-heavy") to competition at the infrastructure layer ("back-end-heavy"). The development path of DeFi lending protocols is evolving from single liquidity pools to layered architectures. The launch of Laniakea is essentially Sky's attempt to secure a niche in the infrastructure layer. Once Laniakea becomes the preferred entry point for 300 billion idle stablecoins, Sky will also upgrade to a central node for on-chain capital allocation. It is worth noting that Sky's transformation is not without risk: a secondary game of governance: although the rules will lock in spending ratios, the power to modify the "rules themselves" still rests with the governance vote. If a governance attack occurs, the long-term effectiveness of the rules may be questionable; Increased technical complexity: Building machine-readable infrastructure that supports real-time AI monitoring is highly challenging. Any vulnerability can be amplified in large-scale deployments; Agent delegation risk: Primes holds significant capital allocation power. Although there is a loss accountability mechanism, in special circumstances, the distribution of profits between the agent and the protocol may still face both legal and technical challenges. From a stablecoin issuer to building the on-chain capital hub Laniakea, Sky is about to complete a transformation from a single DeFi protocol to an institutional-grade operating system. As institutional capital flows in through standardized interfaces, Sky will embark on a new journey.

data.title: What exactly did DeepSeek's newly deleted paper say?
data.description:
Last night, DeepSeek multimodal researcher Chen Xiaokang tweeted on X, announcing DeepSeek's new paper on multimodal technology, "Thinking with Visual Primitives," stating "Excited to release." This morning, the tweet was deleted, and the paper was also removed from GitHub.

But APPSO read the entire paper before it disappeared. After reading it, they felt that the paper's retraction might not be due to problems with its content.

On the contrary, it might have revealed too much.

The day before yesterday, we just tested DeepSeek's image recognition mode, asking it to count fingers. It thought for a while, then complained, "I'm really dizzy from counting," and then answered incorrectly. At the time, we thought it was a minor issue in the gray-testing phase.

This paper tells us that the problem of getting dizzy counting fingers hides a technical bottleneck that GPT, Claude, and Gemini collectively failed to solve. DeepSeek's solution, however, is almost ridiculously simple: Give AI a finger.

Chen Xiaokang wrote in that tweet:

"Traditional CoT stays in the linguistic space, but visual reasoning needs more. By using points and boxes as cognitive anchors, our model bridges the Reference Gap—mimicking the "point-to-reason" synergy humans use."

Compare: For the same 800×800 graph, Gemini-3-Flash consumes approximately 1100 tokens to represent the graph, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 approximately 870, and GPT-5.4 approximately 740. DeepSeek only uses 90 information units in the final calculation. While others use over a thousand grids to remember a graph, DeepSeek only needs 90 grids, and then uses the freed-up computing power for "pointing".

Compare: For the same 800×800 graph, Gemini-3-Flash consumes approximately 1100 tokens to represent the graph, Claude-Sonnet-4.6 approximately 870, and GPT-5.4 approximately 740. DeepSeek only uses 90 information units in the final calculation.

How does it count after being given a finger?

Count 25 people

Give the model a group photo of a football team and ask, "How many people are in the picture?"

Thinking process: First, determine "This is a team photo, so we need to count everyone, including players and coaches." Then, output the coordinates of 25 boxes at once, with a box around each person.

Thinking process: First, determine "This is a team photo, so we need to count everyone, including players and coaches." Then, output the coordinates of 25 boxes at once, with a box around each person.

Next, count by row: 4 in the front row + 9 in the middle row + 8 in the back row + 2 coaches on the left + 2 coaches on the right = 25. "How many bears are on the ground?" There are three bears in the picture. The model frames each bear and determines its position: the first bear is climbing vertically on a tree trunk, so it's excluded; the second bear is walking on the edge of a rock, so it's counted; the third bear is among the wood chips and dirt, so it's counted. Answer: 2 bears. Instead of counting three and subtracting one, it checks each one to see if it's on the ground, with each check having a specific coordinate anchor. It truly checks each one individually, not just guesses. Multi-hop spatial reasoning. A 3D rendered scene contains a bunch of colorful geometric shapes. Question: "Does a purple rubber object exist that is the same size as the gray metal object?" The model first outlines the gray metal sphere, confirming it's a small object. Then, it outlines other small objects in the scene one by one: a brown metal cylinder, a blue metal cube, a blue rubber cube, a yellow rubber cylinder… Each of the six objects is checked individually, verifying its color, material, and size. Conclusion: There is no purple rubber object. Six localization steps and six judgment steps. Each step is anchored by coordinates, preventing situations like "Wait, where were we?" More case studies in the paper: Maze Navigation: While others flip coins, DeepSeek actually searches. The paper tested four tasks, and the maze showed the biggest difference in performance. The task is straightforward: given a maze map, ask if there's a path from the starting point to the end point, and if so, draw it. Mazes come in three shapes: square, circular, and honeycomb. The way the model navigates a maze is similar to how you used to draw on paper with a pencil as a child: choose a fork in the road, go to the end, and if it doesn't work, go back and try another path. The difference is that it marks a coordinate point on the map after each step, leaving a record. The paper demonstrates the complete process of a circular maze: the model first marks the starting and ending points, and then begins its exploration. After 18 steps, it twice entered dead ends and then retreated, finally finding a path and outputting the coordinate points of the entire path. DeepSeek also designed a series of trap mazes: at first glance, there seems to be a path, but a section in the middle is secretly blocked. These mazes test patience; the model cannot just look at the trend near the starting point and draw conclusions; it must honestly try all the possible paths to confirm that it is impassable.

Accuracy comparison:

- DeepSeek: 66.9%

- GPT-5.4: 50.6%

- Claude-Sonnet-4.6: 48.9%

- Gemini-3-Flash: 49.4%

Can understand humor: The natural spots on the cut surface of a piece of fruit happen to form the shape of a melancholy cat's face. The model can point out the similarities and explain why it's funny.

Can provide escape room guidance: Frame the key on the high place, the chair on the floor, and the locked door, and suggest "Move the chair under the key → Stand on it to get the key → Go open the door." The paper frankly describes what it currently cannot do. The input resolution is limited. The ViT output is stuck between 81 and 384 visual information units, and the coordinate precision is insufficient for very detailed scenes (such as counting fingers). This is likely the direct reason why the finger-counting problem failed during the test the other day. Currently, specific trigger words are needed to activate the visual primitive mode. The model cannot yet determine "I should use my fingers to do this problem" on its own; it needs to be prompted. Topological reasoning has limited generalization ability. It works well on familiar maze types, but may fail when faced with new spatial structures. In that now-deleted tweet, Chen Xiaokang also said: "We're still in the early stages; generalization in complex topological reasoning tasks isn't perfect yet, but we're committed to solving it." The capabilities demonstrated by DeepSeek's image recognition mode during the test two days ago (questioning the publisher's identity, associating the meaning of the whale logo, self-correction, and holding a "mini-defense meeting" for itself) are consistent with the thinking method described in this paper. It establishes visual anchors in the brain, reasons around these anchors, and backtracks to correct any contradictions encountered.

The confusion caused by counting fingers is a living demonstration of the Reference Gap. In a scene where fingers are interlaced, relying solely on verbal descriptions to distinguish between "the third finger from the left" and "the second finger from the right" is like trying to count a group of people huddled together without using your own fingers—it's destined to be chaotic.

This paper points to the direction where the next evolution of multimodal reasoning lies in anchoring mechanisms. DeepSeek achieved the same results as others using thousands of tokens with only 90 information units, using the saved computing power to allow the model to "think and point simultaneously."

The resolution arms race can wait; teaching a model to extend its fingers is more effective than giving it a more expensive pair of glasses. This whale, after gaining eyes, even grew fingers. A 66.9% maze accuracy rate is far from perfect, but at least it's navigating seriously, unlike those other models flipping a coin.

data.title: Kelp fulfills its commitment to contribute to the recovery fund; Alphabet's stock price rises to 10%.
data.description:
Headline

▌Kelp: Fulfilled Recovery Fund Donation Commitment, rsETH Reserve Support to be Gradually Restored

On May 1st, Kelp confirmed that it had fulfilled its recovery fund donation commitment, stating that as donations from various parties in DeFi United, led by Aave, have been received, rsETH reserve support will be gradually restored. Kelp donated 2000 ETH from its vault to DeFi United in a one-time donation, aiming to restore the notional exchange rate of rsETH and enable the system to fully recover. The rsETH recovery plan includes funding the cross-chain lockbox, restoring oracle functionality, and cleaning up deficits in affected markets. Kelp expressed its gratitude to partners involved in this recovery fund, including Mantle, Consensys, Arbitrum, ether.fi, Lido Finance, LayerZero, and Compound, and promised to continue to update the community on the progress.

...

▌Alphabet's stock price gains widen to 10%, marking the largest increase in over a year

On May 1st, Alphabet's stock price gains widened to 10%, marking the largest increase in over a year.

**Market Updates** As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $75,785.94, down 0.6% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $2,254.91, down 1.4% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $617.75, down 1.0% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $82.94, down 1.1% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.1038, up 4.6% in the last 24 hours. XRP price is $1.37, a 24-hour change of -0.9%; TRX price is $0.323, a 24-hour change of +0.1%; WLFI price is $0.06335, a 24-hour change of -14.1%; HYPE price is $39.91, a 24-hour change of -0.4%. Policy ▌US Crypto Market Structure Bill Imminent, Ethical Controversy Key On May 1st, the US cryptocurrency market structure legislation process accelerated, with Congress pushing for it to enter the review stage as early as possible. However, ethical issues and the cryptocurrency interests related to Donald Trump still bring uncertainty to the path of progress. One of the main proponents, Senator Thom Tillis, stated that he has asked Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott to schedule hearings and advance the revision and voting process, while also hoping to release the new bill text before deliberation. However, Tillis emphasized that he will oppose the bill's passage if it does not include explicit ethical provisions. With the midterm elections approaching, related ethical issues are expected to become a key focus for the Democratic Party. On May 1st, a notice revealed that the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) formally finalized a regulation on Thursday aimed at replacing the Biden administration's requirement for banks to collect statistics on small business borrowers. Banking lobbying groups had previously stated that this regulation, passed in 2023 during the Biden administration, constitutes an intrusive burden and could ultimately lead to a decrease in small business lending. The Trump administration had previously postponed its effective date multiple times, citing legal challenges facing the regulation. Blockchain Applications

Blockchain Applications

▌Exponent Raises $5 Million Seed Funding Round Led by Multicoin Capital

On April 30th, Exponent Finance, a yield trading platform based on Solana, announced the completion of a $5 million seed funding round, led by Multicoin Capital, with participation from Solana Ventures, RockawayX, L1D, Prelude, and Theia Blockchain, as well as several key figures from the Solana ecosystem as angel investors. This round of financing was initiated in May of last year and closed in August, bringing Exponent's total funding to $7.1 million. Exponent stated that the funds will be used to expand its yield trading platform and upgrade it to a more complete on-chain yield infrastructure, covering the active yield management needs of the Solana ecosystem.

Cryptocurrency ▌Bitwise CIO: Institutional Adoption of Bitcoin is Accelerating and Could Be the Driving Force for the Next Surge On May 1st, Bitwise CIO Matt Hougan stated that institutional adoption of Bitcoin is accelerating, driven by ETFs and large institutions such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs. He also explained the factors that could potentially drive the next significant surge in Bitcoin's price. Syndicate Labs Suffers Private Key Leak Attack, Cross-Chain Bridge Maliciously Upgraded, Resulting in the Transfer of Approximately 18.5 Million SYND

On May 1st, Syndicate Labs disclosed a security incident: attackers compromised the system through a leaked private key and maliciously upgraded the cross-chain bridge contracts on two chains, resulting in the transfer of approximately 18.5 million SYND and approximately $50,000 in user assets. This attack originated from a compromised development endpoint. The attackers used production environment privileges to upgrade the bridge contract to a malicious version, but other chains were unaffected. Losses include: Commons Bridge: Approximately 18.5 million SYND were transferred and sold, equivalent to approximately $330,000; Appchain: Approximately $50,000 in user assets were transferred.

Syndicate Labs stated that affected SYND holders will receive full compensation, plus additional overpayments, resulting in an overall holding higher than pre-event levels; Appchain users affected will also receive full compensation for their losses.

    ▌Bubblemaps: 8360 Wallets Receive MEGA Token Allocation, Half Hold and Unsold

    On May 1st, according to data released by the on-chain visualization and analysis platform Bubblemaps, a total of 8360 wallets received MEGA token airdrop allocations. Of these, 50% of the wallets still hold the tokens, 40% have sold all of them, and 10% have partially sold them. The current fully diluted value (FDV) of the tokens is $1.7 billion.

    ... On May 1st, the Arbitrum DAO launched a governance vote to release 30,766 ETH previously frozen to support DeFi United, a post-Kelp attack recovery plan. These assets, valued at approximately $71.1 million, were frozen by the Arbitrum Security Committee on April 20th and were originally funds transferred to the Arbitrum network by the attackers. If the proposal passes, this will be the largest single investment in the DeFi United plan. Initially, 16.9 million ARBs supported the proposal, and there are currently no opposing votes. Voting will continue until May 7th. **Important Economic Developments** **▌US Debt Exceeds 100% of GDP for the First Time Since World War II** **According to Cointelegraph, US debt has exceeded 100% of GDP for the first time since World War II.** **▌Trump: Doesn't Care if Powell Remains on the Board** **On May 1st, US President Trump stated that he doesn't care if Powell remains on the Federal Reserve Board after his term as Chairman ends. Powell's term as Chairman ends on May 15th, but his term on the Board runs until 2028. He stated that he intends to "continue serving on the Board for an indefinite period." Meanwhile, Kevin Warsh is expected to receive full Senate approval before Powell's term ends, and Powell's continued tenure could complicate Trump's efforts to reshape the Federal Reserve.** When asked if he was prepared to take any action regarding Powell's decision to remain on the board, Trump replied, "No, I don't care if he stays. I just want to make sure Kevin can hold the position." On May 1st, U.S. mortgage rates rose for the first time in a month, even before the crucial spring sales season had begun to pick up. Freddie Mac reported on Thursday that the average rate on a 30-year fixed mortgage rose to 6.3% from 6.23% last week. A year ago, the figure was 6.76%. The housing market has been stagnant since mortgage rates surged in 2022. Before the Iran-Iraq War, rates had fallen below 6%, briefly raising hopes for at least a slight market rebound. Now, geopolitical turmoil is influencing the decisions of both buyers and sellers. On May 1, US President Donald Trump stated at a White House press conference that recent US military operations had "destroyed" Iran's nuclear capabilities and significantly weakened its overall military strength. He claimed that Iran's navy and air force were "virtually nonexistent," approximately 82% of its drone factories were weakened, nearly 90% of its missile factories were damaged, and most missile systems were destroyed. Trump stated that the US completed key strike missions in a short period of time and emphasized that without action, Iran "could acquire nuclear weapons," which "must never be allowed to happen." Trump stated that current negotiations with Iran are stalled, but Iran "very much wants to reach an agreement." He claimed that the details of the negotiations are only known to a very small number of high-ranking US officials. Trump said that under economic sanctions and military pressure, Iran's economy is facing serious difficulties, with a sharp decline in oil revenues. He emphasized that the US goal is to force Iran to abandon its nuclear weapons development while pushing for a swift resolution to the situation through negotiations. (CCTV News) According to CME's "FedWatch" on May 1st, the probability of the Federal Reserve maintaining interest rates unchanged in June is 95%, with a 5% probability of a cumulative rate cut of 25 basis points. The probability of the Fed maintaining interest rates unchanged by July is 87.9%, with an 11.7% probability of a cumulative rate cut of 25 basis points and a 0.4% probability of a cumulative rate cut of 50 basis points. On May 1st, local time on the 30th, the Iranian Securities Exchange Organization announced that, according to a resolution of the Supreme Securities Committee, stock market trading will be suspended until May 4th. The organization stated that the reopening time of the stock market will depend on the results of a continued assessment of market conditions and whether relevant conditions are met. (CCTV News)

    Golden Encyclopedia

    ▌Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Under Geopolitical Crises?

    Every geopolitical crisis causes gold prices to rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

    Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "useful asset in times of crisis," and it can indeed play a role when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries are reduced, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold."

    Generational shifts and the widespread application of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Why is Switzerland indispensable for global digital assets?
data.description:
Author: insights4vc Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Switzerland's role in the digital asset space is often misunderstood as its early affinity for cryptocurrencies. But the more important story lies in the sequence of its institutional development. Before most jurisdictions had even established the legal status of tokens, Switzerland had already provided the protocol foundation, regulated crypto banks, tokenized securities laws, built the market infrastructure for distributed ledger technology (DLT), and conducted practical experiments with digital settlements in the Swiss franc.

The first institutional level is the rise of Zug as a "crypto valley." Zug's low taxes, business-friendly environment, decentralized Swiss politics, and exceptionally open local government made it highly attractive to early blockchain teams from around the world. Since July 2016, the city of Zug has accepted Bitcoin for some municipal service fees, and subsequently conducted experiments such as blockchain-based digital identities. These initiatives, both symbolically and practically, demonstrate that Swiss public institutions are willing to adopt this technology early on, rather than viewing it as a marginal outlier.

Public signals reinforce the formation of private clusters.

On May 3, 2016, the city announced that it would accept Bitcoin payments for some municipal service fees starting July 1, 2016, with an initial limit of 200 Swiss francs. Later that year, the city decided to continue with this arrangement. In 2017, the city moved beyond symbolic gestures and launched a blockchain-based digital identity pilot project; in 2018, it conducted a blockchain-based consultation vote. While these pilot projects did not spawn a large-scale blockchain retail economy, they were significant for the emerging cluster: they demonstrated the local government's willingness to learn through practice. Zug's rise also exhibited path dependence. Once one or two notable projects were established there, others were more likely to follow suit. Lawyers, service providers, trustees, accountants, and bankers became familiar with the field. Entrepreneurs also found established local peers, investors, and advisors. In game theory terms, Zug became a coordinating center because enough early participants believed that other participants would also see it as a coordinating center. This is the deeper significance of Crypto Valley: it's not just a place with good marketing; it has become a common answer, resolving the institutional status of legally ambiguous global agreements.

The blockchain cluster eventually expanded beyond Zug.

A 2025 company report released by CV VC shows that Switzerland and Liechtenstein have a total of 1,749 active blockchain companies, with Zug remaining the core, accounting for 41%, and Zurich ranking second, accounting for 15%. CV VC states that by April 2026, the number of companies in the Crypto Valley will approach 1,800, accounting for 47% of blockchain funding in Europe. In other words, Zug is the starting point for blockchain development, but Switzerland's blockchain ecosystem has expanded nationwide.

Ethereum, Foundations, and Legal Packaging

The early protocol era required a legal framework because even if the protocol community was theoretically decentralized, it still needed to interact with the off-chain world. Token sale proceeds needed to be received, employment and supplier contracts needed to be signed, funded projects needed to be operated, taxes needed to be paid, trademarks needed to be maintained, and someone needed to deal with banks.

Official announcements from UBS Group, Swiss Post Financial Services, and other institutions indicate that the sandbox project involves UBS Group, Swiss Post Financial Services, Sygnum, Cooperative Bank of Austria, the Central Bank of Zurich, the Swiss Federal Bank, and Swiss Stablecoin AG. These partners will test potential applications of the Swiss Franc stablecoin in a controlled, real-world environment during 2026, with Swiss Stablecoin AG providing the technical issuance infrastructure. The banks' statements are cautious: Switzerland currently does not have a regulated and widely used Swiss Franc stablecoin. In other words, the project is still in the exploratory stage and has not yet been formally launched into the national monetary system. An important detail has been added regarding the Swiss stablecoin. According to Swiss Stablecoin AG, the sandbox token is CHFD, issued and redeemed by its wholly-owned subsidiary CHFD Infrastruktur AG, which is affiliated with a self-regulatory organization recognized by the Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority (FINMA). The company stated that the architecture is not blockchain-related, but the sandbox initially runs on Ethereum, using the ERC-20 token standard. The company also stated that during the sandbox phase, the circulating supply will be limited to below 1 million Swiss francs so that the project can obtain an exemption from the Swiss Fintech Sandbox without bank guarantees. These stablecoins are described as being fully backed by Swiss franc deposits held in regulated Swiss banks and segregated from the issuer's operating funds. Careful wording is crucial: this is not a narrow definition of "bank-issued currency," i.e., tokens minted directly by participating banks as bank liabilities. At this stage, a more accurate understanding is that this is a test of an institutionally backed Swiss franc stablecoin infrastructure. The proof-of-concept for deposit tokens differs. In September 2025, the Swiss Bankers Association stated that PostFinance, Sygnum, and UBS Group completed a feasibility study that, for the first time, utilized bank deposits and a public blockchain to achieve legally binding cross-institutional payments. The results report explains that the proof-of-concept employs an account-based model where on-chain tokens represent payment instructions that trigger off-chain fiat currency transfers via SIC. From a legal perspective, this design is viewed as a digital representation of payment instructions, rather than a new bearer-like debt instrument. In short, deposit tokens are not intended to create a transferable, stablecoin-like pool of Swiss franc debt for the public. It aims to test how programmable commercial bank money conforming to interbank agreements can operate on a blockchain, while actual deposit settlement still relies on banking infrastructure. Wholesale central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) differ further. The first approach of the Helvetia project places tokenized central bank money and tokenized assets on the same distributed ledger technology (DLT) infrastructure, enabling integrated cash-on-delivery (DPV) on the Swiss Stock Exchange (SDX). The second approach uses traditional central bank money to achieve cross-system synchronization of asset and cash settlements through a connection to the Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) system with the Swiss National Bank (SIC). The Swiss National Bank (SNB) 2025 annual report also described a third scenario in its conceptual analysis: a privately-owned tokenized currency protected by bankruptcy and backed by central bank money. Therefore, Switzerland is building a layered digital currency system. Retail or quasi-retail applications may be better suited to regulated stablecoins; interbank and programmable payment applications may favor deposit tokens or tokenized deposit structures; while securities settlement applications may rely on integrated wholesale CBDCs or synchronized central bank money. The key is not convergence to a single tool, but functional coexistence. Lugano Cluster as a Secondary Cluster Lugano should not be confused with Zug, but it holds a place in the history of cryptocurrency development in Switzerland. If Zug is the cluster of infrastructure and protocols, and Zurich is the center of banking and infrastructure, then Lugano positions itself as a city-level experiment in cryptocurrency applications. Through the "Plan ₿" initiative launched in partnership with Tether, Lugano is promoting the use of Bitcoin, USDT, and LVGA for payments, as well as conducting education programs, hosting events, and providing relocation incentives. City government data shows that the second phase of the plan officially launched on March 3, 2026, and will continue until 2030. ... Its official website describes it as a fully regulated, 1:1 collateralized euro stablecoin project backed by leading European banks. Reuters reported in December 2025 that the Amsterdam-based joint venture, comprised of major banks including ING, UniCredit, and BNP Paribas, was applying for electronic money institution status from the Dutch Central Bank and planned a formal launch in 2026. ING and CaixaBank have also mentioned Qivalis as a bank-led euro stablecoin project. Fireblocks stated in April 2026 that it had been selected as Qivalis's infrastructure partner, and the project planned to meet MiCA standards in the second half of 2026. Compared to the Swiss Franc sandbox, Qivalis is more pan-European, more directly affected by MiCA, and has a wider potential distribution range. In contrast, the Swiss sandbox is smaller, more experimental, and more tightly confined to a single financial center. The European Central Bank (ECB) is taking a more cautious approach. Reuters reported in late 2025 that the ECB warned that stablecoins could siphon deposits from eurozone banks and trigger financial stability issues. This caution aligns with Europe's desire to develop a euro-denominated digital currency. This means that Europe's strategy is likely to remain within the framework of the Money Innovation and Application (MiCA), combining public-sector caution with private-sector experimentation. The situation in the United States is quite different. For much of the previous cycle, the US cryptocurrency regulatory system was more decentralized and enforcement-focused than Switzerland's tailored system. But the US has not stopped there. On July 18, 2025, the President signed the GENIUS Act, establishing a federal framework for payments of stablecoins. In April 2026, the Treasury Department proposed rules to implement the Act's anti-money laundering and sanctions program requirements, which apply to approved institutions issuing payments of stablecoins. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Cryptocurrency Working Group, led by Commissioner Hester Pierce, stated that its goal is to clarify the applicability of federal securities laws to crypto assets and to propose practical policy recommendations. This marks a significant step towards standardization in U.S. cryptocurrency regulation. Switzerland, therefore, lies between two systems. It lacks the regulatory scale of the EU and the depth of the U.S. capital markets, venture capital ecosystem, and dollar network effects. However, it made targeted adjustments earlier, which helped it build a vibrant institutional infrastructure before the EU and the U.S. had finalized the classification of many such activities. This is Switzerland's comparative advantage: precision before scale. The main takeaway is that the next phase of digital assets is less about a boom in consumer tokens and more about competition around regulatory tracks. In Switzerland, at least five tracks warrant attention: integrated custody and trading by banks; issuance and distribution of tokenized securities; distributed ledger technology (DLT) trading venues; tokenized commercial bank money; and settlement in or with central bank currency. The Swiss ecosystem has already implemented solutions in each of these categories. For institutional investors, business signals are not only reflected in asset prices or venture capital rounds, but also in whether regulators are continuously increasing services and the actual activity of clients. PostFinance and ZKB's product expansion, corporate client acceptance, and any moves beyond existing asset classes are worth watching. Companies like Sygnum, AMINA, Taurus, and Crypto Finance, as "gold diggers" in the financial sector, should also be closely monitored. The issuer quality, trading volume, secondary market participation, and settlement reliability of SDX and BX Digital are also worth noting. For venture capitalists and fintech operators, the more attractive opportunities may lie in interoperability, workflow software, fund management tools, and compliance infrastructure, rather than speculative token consumption applications. Deposit tokens, stablecoins, and wCBDCs are not mutually exclusive; rather, they create a need for coordination: wallet control, authorization lists, audit trails, identity verification, fund management, tokenization middleware, and the legal and operational transitions between traditional ledger and distributed ledger technology (DLT) environments. Switzerland is attractive in this regard due to the exceptionally high concentration of institutional users, banking partners, and regulators. The key points to watch over the next 12 to 24 months are clear. First, whether the Swiss franc stablecoin sandbox can generate credible use cases and eventually move beyond a controlled environment. Second, whether the deposit token concept can evolve from the proof-of-concept stage to broader bank participation. Third, whether the Helvetia project can continue to expand, and whether BX Digital's SIC-linked model can achieve substantial adoption. Fourth, can SDX trading be deepened through higher-value issuers and more active secondary market trading? Fifth, how will the implementation of MiCA, Qivalis, and the US GENIUS rules reshape the competitive pressure on related initiatives in Switzerland? These indicators will tell investors whether Switzerland will continue to maintain its niche pioneer status or become a lasting model for the regulated digital finance sector.

Headline

Hyperliquid Proposes to Launch Prediction Market Feature

On April 30th, decentralized cryptocurrency exchange Hyperliquid proposed adding a prediction market feature to its platform, directly competing with Kalshi and Polymarket. The proposal, numbered HIP-4, is currently in public testing and will allow users to place bets on the outcomes of real-world events on the Hyperliquid platform. Hyperliquid is one of the most active trading venues in the current digital asset space, and the speed and scale of its product expansion has attracted widespread attention from Wall Street.

AI Hot Topics 1. The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) launched a special campaign, "Clean Up the Internet and Rectify the Chaos in AI Applications," on April 30. 2. SoftBank's $40 billion loan for OpenAI equity has attracted more banks to participate in the joint underwriting of the deal, according to reports on April 30. These include HSBC Holdings plc, BNP Paribas, and Intesa Sanpaolo.

3. US AI Giants Wave Their Checkbooks; Analysts: Supply Chain Faces "All-Round Bottlenecks"

On April 30th, $725 billion—this is the total capital expenditure ceiling for the four tech giants, Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Amazon, this year. Behind this "accelerated" capital expenditure, each giant is facing constraints on hardware infrastructure and pressure from rising prices: Microsoft stated that of its $190 billion in capital expenditure, $25 billion is used to cope with rising component prices; Google stated that its capital expenditure this quarter was $35.7 billion, including real estate, servers, data centers, and other infrastructure; Meta attributed its increased capital expenditure to higher hardware component prices this year, as well as the additional data center costs needed to support future capacity.

... Jefferies technology analysts stated, "What we're seeing is a comprehensive bottleneck. Hyperscale cloud service providers either need to wait or have to pay higher costs to enter the market. This is good for the 'shovel sellers,' but not good for companies that need to integrate all the links." Cambricon's stock price hits a new high, with market capitalization exceeding 670 billion yuan. On April 30th, Cambricon's stock price rose nearly 13% intraday, breaking through 1595.88 yuan per share, setting a new historical high, and its market capitalization exceeded 670 billion yuan. Previously, Cambricon's stock price surpassed Yuanjie Technology, once again becoming the new stock king of the A-share market. 5. China Bans Manus Acquisition, Signaling Compliance for Key Transactions On April 30th, according to CCTV, industry lawyers stated that the Manus acquisition involved transferring domestic AI business assets overseas and ultimately selling them to the overseas company Meta. According to the "Measures for Foreign Investment Security Review," even if the initial relocation occurred between related entities controlled by the founders, subsequent transactions will still be subject to foreign investment security review. Furthermore, Manus moved its headquarters to Singapore, while its core business remained in China. Subsequently, the company gradually transferred key personnel, technology, and other critical assets related to its core business overseas. Simultaneously, the domestic Manus company was gradually separated from its core business, retaining only non-core business operations. The entire operation ultimately achieved the complete transfer of the core business of the Manus group from China to overseas. Lawyers stated that any attempt to circumvent regulations through structural design is non-compliant, and compliance is the lifeline of a company. Shen Ziying, a senior partner at Beijing Jincheng Tongda Law Firm, stated that this sends a signal, indicating where the red line for government regulation lies. National security review and regulation follow the principle of "substance over form," so I think this draws a red line.

DeFi Hot Topics

1. Berachain Foundation: Wasabi Reward Vault Suspended

On April 30th, the Berachain Foundation announced that Wasabi had been hacked on all chains (including Berachain). Users with funds in Wasabi should withdraw them immediately. Berachain users have approximately $50,000 in risk exposure, and the Wasabi reward vault has been suspended.

...

2. DeSci Project NanoVita Announces Strategic Financing Completion, Paramita and Others Participate

On April 30th, the DeSci project NanoVita officially announced the completion of strategic financing, the amount of which was not disclosed. This round of financing was led by Paramita, Harbor Digital Ventures, and Apus Capital. NanoVita is a decentralized science (DeSci) protocol that integrates nanotechnology, AI bio-intelligence, and real-world health data, aiming to build an open and transparent health research infrastructure. In addition, NanoVita had previously completed a seed round of financing with participation from Cryptogram Venture (CGV), UDS Space, Candaq Fintech Group, and Susie Venture, as well as a Pre-A round of financing with participation from K24 Ventures, LandScape Capital, and WestLabs.

3. Tether Mints 1 Billion USDT on Tron Network

On April 30th, at 15:45:00 Beijing time, Tether minted 1,000,000,000 USDT on the Tron network.

4. South Korean Crypto Exchange Bithumb Announces Introduction of Quantum-Resistant Cryptography Technology

On April 30th, according to the Korea Economic Daily, South Korean cryptocurrency exchange Bithumb announced the introduction of quantum-resistant cryptography technology to prevent the risk that future quantum computers may break existing encryption systems. Bithumb plans to fully apply PQC technology from virtual asset wallet management to identity authentication systems, focusing on protecting customer private keys, and will cooperate with security technology company ATON to encrypt all aspects of data processing, including input, authentication, transmission, and storage.

data.title: Visa has incorporated stablecoins and Agentic Commerce into its financial statements.
data.description:
On April 29th, Visa released its Q2 2026 financial report (starting from October of the fiscal year). Net revenue increased by 17% year-on-year, and EPS increased by 20%, marking its strongest quarter since 2013. CEO McInerney defined Visa's future in three points during the conference call: First, AI and agentic commerce will expand Visa's addressable market; second, stablecoins and blockchain represent significant opportunities, and Visa has established itself as a "key interoperability layer" between on-chain infrastructure and the real world; third, these investments and innovations are yielding meaningful returns. When the world's largest payments network makes stablecoins and agentic commerce the core narrative of its financial report, rather than the "innovation exploration" section in the appendix, the industry inflection point no longer needs prediction—it has already occurred. This monthly report covers three sectors: stablecoin payments, on-chain finance (focusing on the actions of financial institutions), and agentic commerce. Below is the complete picture for April. The financial system is being rewired both externally (stablecoins) and internally (tokenized deposits and securities). AI agents will be among the first large-scale users of this new wiring. April Stablecoin Market Snapshot Stablecoin supply is a fundamental measure of on-chain economic activity. It's not just the market capitalization of an asset class—stablecoins serve as a medium of exchange for almost all on-chain financial scenarios, including trading, payments, lending, and settlement. Observing changes in stablecoin supply is essentially observing the speed of the on-chain economy's blood circulation.

I. Stablecoin Payments

1.1 US Regulation: From Legislative Completion to Rule Implementation

The US regulatory landscape in April consisted of three lines: the GENIUS Act governing issuance, the SEC Reg Crypto governing asset classification, and OCC trust banks governing custody and settlement.

The surge in OCC trust bank licenses is the most noteworthy signal.

Within 83 days, Circle, Ripple, BitGo, Paxos, Fidelity Digital Assets, Bridge (Stripe), Crypto.com, Protego, Morgan Stanley, Payoneer, and Zerohash received approval or submitted applications. Coinbase joined the list on April 2nd. The license allows for unified operation nationwide as a federally qualified custodian—but prohibits accepting deposits or making loans. Institutions must establish a federally compliant structure before the GENIUS Act takes effect. Morgan Stanley MSNXX: Invests only in ≤93-day Treasury bonds and overnight repos, $1 NAV, minimum $10 million, fee rate 0.15%. Circle or Paxos can directly purchase to meet GENIUS Act reserve obligations. Simultaneously, it launched a DAP Class tokenized share in partnership with BNY. Stablecoin regulation is shifting from a "crypto issue" to a "bank asset management issue." Banks are simultaneously lobbying for a ban on non-bank stablecoins paying interest in the Clarity Act. The ABA warns that yield-paying stablecoins lead to deposit outflows. White House analysis shows that limiting yields only increases bank lending by 0.02%. If the ban passes, the real battle becomes "who gets the reserve asset yields": issuers cannot pay interest to holders, but the $320 billion+ in stablecoins backed by government bonds continues to accrue interest—platform rewards, loyalty perks, and better pricing will become the battleground for "indirect profits." This is not regulatory neutrality. This is a traditional move by banks to protect deposits through legislation, only the opponent has changed to stablecoins. 1.2 Global Licenses: Six Jurisdictions Enter Operational Phase The US GENIUS Act, Europe's MiCA, Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance, Singapore's MAS framework, UAE's four-tier framework, and Japan's PSA amendment—major global jurisdictions have successively completed legislation. April marked the beginning of license issuance and product launches. Hong Kong—HKMA issued its first batch of stablecoin licenses on April 10: HSBC and Anchorpoint Financial (a joint venture of Standard Chartered, Animoca, and HKT). Of the 36 applications received, the first two were approved. They will issue Hong Kong dollar stablecoins for cross-border payments and RWA settlements. Regulators are betting on large banks. Europe – The Qivalis consortium (including 12 banks such as UniCredit) selected Fireblocks to build the MiCA compliant Euro stablecoin, launching in H2 2026. However, industry reports indicate that MiCA makes Euro stablecoins "safe but commercially uncompetitive"—full reserves and zero interest make Euro EMT uncompetitive. Trading volume increased by 1,200%, but still less than 0.3% of global VASP. It has the most robust regulatory framework but the weakest commercial viability. UK – The FCA released crypto boundary guidelines on April 15th, and the Treasury proposed exemptions for stablecoin payments. The license application window opened on September 30th. UAE – CBUAE manages local currency stablecoins, while VARA/ADGM/DIFC manage foreign currency and service providers. The "federal government regulates domestic currency, free zones regulate foreign currency" model creates competitive arbitrage opportunities, making the UAE a key node in the Middle East-South Asia cross-border corridor. Paxos received MAS approval to operate the first compliant stablecoin. South Korea drafted legislation to include stablecoins. Israel approved its first shekel stablecoin, BILS. Pakistan requires prior approval for virtual asset pilot programs. The maturity indicator is no longer "whether there is a law," but "whether products under the law can function." 1.3 The Geographic Explosion of Stablecoins: From Dollar Tools to Multi-Currency Infrastructure Brazil's Q1 stablecoin purchases reached $6.8 billion, accounting for 98% of all crypto transactions. Lula suspended stablecoin taxes before the election. Japan's JPYC surpassed $100 million in payments on Polygon (only 8 months after its launch). South Korea's KB National Card tests a hybrid stablecoin credit card on Avalanche, while Axelar completes its Proof-of-Concept (PoC) for a Korean won stablecoin. The Iran war is driving commodity traders to stablecoins—Western banks are de-banking traders, with USDT filling a gap in the $2 trillion non-bank trade finance market. Ripple's RLUSD has a market capitalization of $1.5 billion. VALR + Onafriq enables mobile payments for crypto assets in 43 African countries. Despite the coexistence of multiple currencies, the US dollar remains the center of gravity. The vast majority of Brazil's $6.8 billion in stablecoins is in USDT and USDC.

1.4 Infrastructure Competition: Card Networks · Cross-border Giants · Stablecoin Native Platforms

Visa: $7 Billion Stablecoin Settlement + Nine Chains + Financial Report Confirms Economic Model

Q2 Financial Report: Stablecoin card payment volume YoY +200%, card projects increased to 160+, settlement annualized $7 billion (QoQ +50%). Nine new chains were added (Arc/Base/Canton/Polygon/Tempo), with settlement nodes deployed on the chains of each major stablecoin issuer. McInerney confirmed that the unit economic model is "very similar" to traditional products, positioning Visa as a "hyperscaling bridge layer." Visa + WeFi is testing non-custodial wallet stablecoin daily consumption.

Mastercard's $1.8 billion acquisition of BVNK is under review. BVNK covers 130+ countries and offers 24/7 stablecoin settlement. MC + KuCoin supports USDC spending in Australia. PayPal's PYUSD has expanded to 70 markets, but 91% of its $4 billion supply is in DeFi yield farms (16-18% APY), with near-zero merchant adoption. When DeFi yields are used to pay out returns, capital inevitably flows to yield. Western Union's May launch of USDPT should serve as a cautionary tale. Circle CPN Managed Payments (4/8) – Institutions can access USDC settlement without holding digital assets. USDC has settled over $70 trillion. David Marcus launched a stablecoin banking platform through Lightspark. dLocal Stablecoin Full API covers 44+ emerging markets. Coinbase + Nium USDC settles in 190+ countries. MetaMask launches $mUSD—the first wallet-native stablecoin (backed by government bonds), transforming the largest self-custodied wallet from a "tool" to an "issuer." Tether expands into emerging markets—Gold Visa card (Fasset, 125 countries) + led a $14 million investment in Belo to expand into 6 Latin American countries. Locking in the distribution network in regions with the highest USDT penetration. Y Combinator's first all-stablecoin funding round—$500,000 USDC, settled in three Solana transactions at a cost of <1 cent. All YC companies can receive investments in stablecoins. This is not an ideological signal, but a cost calculation. Stablecoins are being absorbed into existing payment infrastructure. The front end consists of PayPal wallets, Visa cards, and the dLocal API; the back end is on-chain settlement. 1.5 USDT vs. USDC: A Trust Fork Triggered by an Exploit Drift Protocol suffered a loss of over $270 million due to a North Korean hack on April 1st. The attackers transferred $232 million USDC via CCTP within 8 hours; Circle did not freeze it. Tether intervened, providing up to $127.5 million, and the Drift settlement layer switched from USDC to USDT. However, at the end of April, Tether itself froze $344 million USDt (at the request of law enforcement). USDT reached a new high of $188 billion, and USDC reached $78.25 billion. Compass Point gave Circle a "sell" rating. Tether opted for a "rescue + locked settlement layer" approach; Circle's "no freeze" came at the cost of its share. Are stablecoin issuers "neutral infrastructure" or "quasi-regulators"? The GENIUS Act will force a response. 1.6 Market Forecasts: Between Optimism and Discouragement Chainalysis predicts stablecoins could reach $1.5 quadrillion by 2035. Juniper Research predicts cross-border B2B stablecoins will grow from $13.4 billion to $5 trillion (+37,000%). FinTech Weekly predicts they will account for 3% of USD payments in 2026 and 10% in 2031. American Banker pours cold water: the scale of real goods and services payments is limited. Former IMF and Fed Vice Chair Barr emphasizes reserve and regulatory risks. Even looking only at B2B cross-border transactions—McKinsey $226 billion, +733% YoY—the growth signal is clear enough. II. On-Chain Finance 2.1 Data First: Signals of RWA Tokenization RWA.xyz: Distributed asset value $30.52 billion, a 9.19% increase in 30 days, with over 735,000 holding addresses. Tokenized government bonds approach $14 billion, BlackRock BUIDL ~$5.2 billion. Ondo holds 61% of tokenized shares, adding proxy voting rights to $700 million of tokenized shares in April. Coinbase-EY: 67% of institutions prioritize tokenization within two years. The increase in holding addresses almost entirely comes from "purpose-oriented wallets." RWA is the primary entry point for institutions to go on-chain—not because it's the most advanced, but because it's the most boring and the most trustworthy. 2.2 Wall Street Deployment: From Lab to Product Line Dimon's annual shareholder letter (4/6): "Tokenization is a direct competitor to banks." Canton Network processes over $6 trillion, with on-chain transaction volume increasing 30-fold since 2023. Morgan Stanley released three announcements in January: MSBT Bitcoin ETF (net inflow of $172 million), MSNXX Reserve Fund, and DAP tokenized shares. Invesco took over management of the Superstate tokenized Treasury bond fund. Nasdaq tokenized stock trading received SEC approval, with a 23-hour trading window launching on December 6th. BNY + Goldman maintains MMF shares on-chain. State Street launched its tokenized fund service in Luxembourg at the end of the year. A division of labor model of "Crypto-native technology development and TradeFi brand operation and distribution" is taking shape. 2.3 Tokenized Deposits vs. Stablecoins: Banks' Institutional Counterattack JPMorgan JPMD listed on Base. BNY launched tokenized deposits for ICE, Citadel, and Circle. Five regional banks launched Cari Network ($600 billion in deposits) on ZKsync. HSBC completed 24/7 atomic settlement in Canton. Japan's six major banks completed instant settlement of security token DVPs using DCJPY, and Japan Post Bank plans to cover 120 million accounts. Canton processes $350 billion daily. Key differences: Tokenized deposits are direct liabilities of banks, insured by the FDIC, and legally pay interest; stablecoins are claims on reserves and are not insured. Banks use tokenized deposits as a systemic weapon—not against blockchain, but against non-bank stablecoins. 2.4 Market Infrastructure Tokenization: Upgrading the Pipeline Layer DTCC's tokenization service received an SEC No-Action Letter and launched in H2 (Russell 1000 + ETF + Treasury bonds). DTCC/Clearstream/Euroclear jointly released an interoperability framework. Clearstream D7 DLT launched. Deutsche Börse invested an additional $200 million. BlackRock BUIDL + Standard Chartered + OKX launched a tokenized collateral framework. SWIFT became a Ripple Treasury ecosystem partner. Chainlink and 24 banks deploy AI Oracles. DTCC clears the vast majority of US securities, and Clearstream holds $21 trillion in custody—tokenization is not a parallel experiment, but an upgrade path for existing infrastructure. 2.5 DeFi Packaging Layer: Middleware for Institutional Access to On-Chain Yields Fireblocks Earn integrates Morpho + Aave, with over a thousand institutions already operating on the platform. Coinbase Morpho USDC lending expands to the UK (over $2.17 billion in loans). Kraken DeFi Vaults reach $200 million. Galaxy leads a $20 million investment in Fence ($6 trillion credit market automation, BBVA/BlackRock/Fortress are already clients). DeFi Institutionalization = DeFi Protocol → Compliance Packaging Layer → Endpoint. The packaging layer is the value capture point. 2.6 Global Framework: IMF Sets the Tone IMF (4/2): Tokenization is a structural restructuring of the financial architecture, "same activity, same risk, same regulation." Hashed + ADGM: Tokenization has entered a "market redesign" phase. Basel risk weights and accounting rules remain major obstacles. Canada's CSA launched Project Tokenization. The Ethereum Foundation completed staking of 70,000 ETH. When the IMF defines tokenization as a "structural restructuring," the space for action for central banks is redefined.

III. Agentic Commerce

During Visa's Q2 earnings call, McInerney outlined four logics for market expansion: agentic commerce accelerates digitalization; agent-split purchases create more transactions; agents pay for data and computation on an event-by-event basis, "creating entirely new micro-transaction categories"; more transactions = more VAS demand.

But the most pointed assessment is the bottleneck: "The limiting factor for agentic commerce is not computing power, not electricity, not data centers—it's trust." He directly compared cards to stablecoins: "Visa cards offer privacy, aggregated liquidity management, KYC, security protection, and rewards. We don't see any other payment method on Earth that can offer all of these simultaneously." AmEx has already assumed agent fraud risk—how will Visa's four-party model follow suit? Response: Rules evolve with the level of ecosystem purchases.

Core Tension: Card networks see trust as a barrier, while the native crypto protocol believes trust is a problem that can be solved by technology.

3.1 Card Network Shift

Visa Intelligent Commerce Connect (4/8): A unified entry point independent of protocols/networks, supporting multiple agent payment protocols. AI dispute resolution + Ramp automated enterprise billing. Mastercard Agent Pay + Verifiable Intent (4/29), contributed to the FIDO Alliance. Agentic payments extended to Hong Kong.

Visa acts as the "router," Mastercard acts as the "standard setter." The same fear: agents can bypass card networks by settling with stablecoins.

Visa acts as the "router," Mastercard acts as the "standard setter."

3.2 Four Major Protocols + FIDO Alliance The FIDO Alliance established two working groups on April 28th (Google contributed AP2, MC contributed Verifiable Intent). The four protocols complement each other: AP2 authorization, ACP settlement, and x402/MPP settlement. The battleground is not "which protocol wins," but "who controls the orchestration layer." 3.3 Demand Side and Trust Bottlenecks PYMNTS/Worldpay: 43% of retailers piloted agents, 45% of consumers were willing to use agents (Generation Z 54%), 95% had concerns, and 50% said fraud protection could improve trust. Projected to reach $1.7 trillion by 2030. 45% willingness vs. 95% concerns confirm McInerney's assessment. However, when AI automatically resolves disputes, the marginal cost of chargebacks approaches zero, eroding the "consumer protection" premium of credit card networks. 3.4 Infrastructure Productization a16z: Non-human identities are already 100 times more advanced than those of humans in the financial industry. x402/MPP processes $0.003 microtransactions. Ant Digital Anvita launches an agent-to-agent platform (in partnership with HSBC/BNP/Circle). Adobe CX Enterprise orchestrates the customer lifecycle. Worldpay + Trulioo agentic KYC compliance layer. Bluefish raises $43 million. Product adaptation is initially focused on B2B. A killer app for B2C is yet to arrive. 3.5 Legal Framework: A Blank Area Fenwick Law Firm published "Is 2026 the Year of Agentic Payments?" on April 23, outlining three layers of legal challenges. Layer 1: Fragmented AI Legislation. There is no unified federal AI law. The Trump administration issued policy framework recommendations but did not cover payment scenarios, and states have begun independent legislation. Companies face a multi-layered compliance puzzle. Layer 2: Payment Licenses. If AI agents hold or transfer funds, it may trigger state-level money transfer license requirements. The federal and state attitudes on this are still unclear. Layer 3 (Most Urgent): Regulation E. The law is based on the premise that "consumers explicitly authorize each transfer," but it doesn't address three scenarios: ① When consumers delegate authorization to an AI agent that can learn autonomously, where are the boundaries of that original authorization? ② Who bears the losses when the agent exceeds its authorization, misunderstands instructions, or initiates unintended transactions—the user, the AI ​​developer, the bank, or the merchant? ③ When an agent makes a purchase on behalf of a user, how does the definition of "unauthorized" apply, and do consumers enjoy traditional dispute resolution rights? Fenwick points out that agentic payments will be subject to a tiered system of AI laws, financial services regulations, and consumer protection laws, and there is currently no mechanism to coordinate the conflicts between these laws. He advises businesses to "plan for the duration of legal uncertainty" rather than waiting for clear rules. It's not an oversight—it's that laws from the 1970s couldn't foresee the realities of 2026.

IV. April Funding

Q1 crypto VC ~$5 billion (YoY -16%), transaction volume -45.9%, deal size +76.4%. April weekly average $40-80M. AI crowding-out effect is significant.

Trends: ① AI crowds out crypto VC, but some VCs begin to "rebalance". ② Traditional capital is locked in compliant infrastructure (Deutsche Börse/T. Rowe Price/Invesco). ③ Rescue funding becomes a new category (Drift $147.5M + DeFi United $300M+).

④ Emerging markets are attracting real money.

V. What the April market tells us

Looking back at all the material from this month, three main themes are taking shape simultaneously.

  • First, stablecoins have moved beyond the crypto context. Visa's $7 billion run rate, Morgan Stanley's reserve fund, Brazil's $6.8 billion quarterly purchase, Western Union's token issuance, Y Combinator's investment in startups with USDC—the common thread in these events is that the participants no longer care about the "crypto" label. They care about settlement speed, cost, and programmability. When Nacha puts stablecoins on its annual conference agenda and the Financial Trade Group offers stablecoin certification courses for treasury professionals, stablecoins are completing their transformation from "alternative assets" to "financial infrastructure." The 91% DeFi dilemma of PYUSD serves as a mirror—supply does not equal adoption; issuing tokens is easy, but getting people to spend them is difficult. However, the 733% YoY increase in B2B cross-border payments indicates that actual adoption on the enterprise side has far exceeded expectations on the retail side. Secondly, traditional finance is not simply "connecting to crypto," but rather rebuilding its settlement layer using blockchain. JPMorgan launched tokenized deposits on Base and Canton, HSBC achieved 24/7 atomic settlement on Canton, Japan's six major banks used DCJPY for instant securities DVP settlement, and Canton processes $350 billion in tokenized value daily—this trend parallels but differs from stablecoins. Stablecoins are external challengers, while tokenized deposits represent internal reform. Banks lobbied in the Clarity Act to prohibit non-bank stablecoins from paying interest, using this very institutional leverage. Two paths will coexist, serving different market segments—but the political battle over "whose money counts as real money" has already begun. The tokenized securities plans of DTCC, Nasdaq, and Clearstream illustrate that the upgrade of the pipeline layer is irreversible. Tokenization is not replacing TradFi, but becoming the new operating system for TradFi. Third, AI agents will become the first large-scale users of this new pipeline. Visa Intelligent Commerce Connect, Mastercard Agent Pay, and the FIDO Alliance Standardization Working Group—all launched simultaneously in April. a16z points out that non-human identities are already 100 times more prevalent than human employees in the financial industry, and x402 and MPP process microtransactions at a fee of $0.003. However, the data gap of 43% pilot vs. 95% with concerns indicates that a killer application for B2C has not yet emerged. True product-market fit will arrive first in B2B: ramp billing automation, enterprise procurement processes, and pay-as-you-go API calls. Fenwick points out the legal loopholes in Regulation E regarding agent overreach—this isn't an oversight, but rather a consequence of 1970s law failing to foresee the realities of 2026. The story on the funding side is even more sobering. AI is eating up the crypto VC lunch, with weekly averages of $40-80M in April contrasting sharply with AI companies' $200M+ seed rounds. However, the entry point for traditional financial capital is very clear—Deutsche Börse $200M, T. Rowe Price, Pantera, Invesco—targeting compliant payments and RWA infrastructure, not crypto narratives. The emergence of "rescue funding" as a new category (Drift $147.5M, DeFi United $300M+) directly prices the composability risk of DeFi. In short: the financial system is being rewired simultaneously, both externally and internally, and AI agents will be among the first to use this new pipeline on a large scale. The question in April was no longer "whether it would happen"—it already was. The question was who controlled the orchestration layer. Visa said it was me, Mastercard said it was me, the FIDO Alliance said let's set the standards together, Coinbase said with x402 no one needs to control it. The answer isn't out yet. But the pipeline is already being laid.
